definition: Unqualified assent to offer terms; creates binding contract upon communication of acceptance to offeror.
federalContext: Acceptance must be unequivocal and on offer terms; conditional acceptance constitutes counteroffer.
dataContext: Acceptance timing and method affect contract formation.
es_definition: Asentimiento inequívoco a los términos de una oferta; crea un contrato vinculante al comunicar la aceptación al oferente.
es_context: En la ley federal, la aceptación debe ser inequívoca y conforme a los términos de la oferta; la aceptación condicionada constituye una contraoferta.

definition: An agreement (accord) and its performance (satisfaction) that resolves a disputed claim, with the satisfaction discharging the original obligation.
federalContext: Governed by state contract law; federal courts recognize accord and satisfaction as valid contract defense.
dataContext: Settlement mechanism in contract disputes.
es_definition: Un acuerdo (acuerdo) y su cumplimiento (satisfacción) que resuelve un reclamo disputado, descargando la obligación original.
es_context: Regido por la ley de contratos estatal; los tribunales federales reconocen el acuerdo y la satisfacción como defensa válida de contrato.

definition: Federal statute prohibiting age discrimination in employment for individuals 40 years of age and older; enforced by EEOC.
federalContext: 29 U.S.C. § 621 et seq.; private right of action; applies to employers with 20+ employees; damages available.
dataContext: Employment discrimination case type; federal question jurisdiction.
es_term: Ley de Discriminación por Edad en el Empleo (ADEA)
es_definition: Estatuto federal que prohíbe la discriminación por edad en el empleo para individuos de 40 años o más; ejecutado por la EEOC.
es_context: 29 U.S.C. § 621 y siguientes; derecho privado de acción; se aplica a empleadores con 20 o más empleados.

definition: A requirement that complainant pursue available administrative remedies before filing federal court suit.
federalContext: Required for employment discrimination claims before filing suit; EEOC charge filing required for Title VII claims.
dataContext: Administrative exhaustion is procedural requirement eliminating premature suits.
es_definition: Requisito de que el demandante agote los recursos administrativos disponibles antes de presentar una demanda en tribunal federal.
es_context: Requerido para reclamos de discriminación en el empleo antes de presentar una demanda; se requiere presentación de cargo ante la EEOC.

definition: Evidence that is legally sufficient and relevant to be presented to a jury or judge at trial.
federalContext: Fed. R. Evid. 402 provides that relevant evidence is generally admissible unless specifically excluded.
dataContext: Evidentiary disputes and admissibility challenges affect trial presentation.
es_definition: Prueba que es legalmente suficiente y relevante para ser presentada ante un jurado o juez en juicio.
es_context: La Regla Federal de Evidencia 402 establece que la prueba relevante es generalmente admisible a menos que se excluya específicamente.

definition: A written statement made under oath before an authorized officer, used in place of testimony.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 56(c) permits affidavits in support of summary judgment motions; must show personal knowledge.
dataContext: Affidavits are common in summary judgment motions and preliminary relief requests.
es_definition: Declaración escrita hecha bajo juramento ante un funcionario autorizado, utilizada en lugar del testimonio.
es_context: La Regla Federal de Procedimiento Civil 56(c) permite declaraciones juradas en apoyo de mociones de sentencia sumaria; debe demostrar conocimiento personal.

definition: An appellate court's decision upholding a lower court judgment; judgment stands unchanged.
federalContext: Affirmation without opinion is common when appellate court agrees with lower court reasoning.
dataContext: Affirmation rates indicate trial court decision quality.
es_definition: Declaración solemne hecha sin juramento pero bajo pena de perjurio, teniendo la misma fuerza legal que un juramento.
es_context: Empleada como alternativa a un juramento formal cuando el individuo tiene objeciones religiosas o de conciencia al juramento.

definition: A defense raising new facts or legal arguments that do not dispute plaintiff's allegations but avoid liability if proven.
federalContext: Examples include statute of limitations, res judicata, comparative negligence, assumption of risk; must be pleaded in answer.
dataContext: Affirmative defense pleadings appear early in litigation.
es_definition: Defensa que reconoce los hechos alegados por el demandante pero presenta otras circunstancias que evitan o mitigan la responsabilidad.
es_context: Ejemplos incluyen prescripción, consentimiento, asunción de riesgo y defensa propia; debe alegarse en la respuesta.

definition: A doctrine of judicial review deferring to agency expertise and interpretations of statutes they administer.
federalContext: Chevron deference requires deference to reasonable agency statutory interpretation; Auer deference applies to agency regulations.
dataContext: Agency deference affects Administrative Procedure Act cases.
es_definition: Principio por el cual los tribunales defieren a las interpretaciones de agencias federales de leyes que administran.
es_context: Aplicado bajo estándares como la deferencia de Chevron en asuntos de regulación federal y cumplimiento normativo.

definition: Latin phrase meaning 'friend of the court'; a person or organization not party to a case who petitions to participate and offer information or perspective to assist the court.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. App. P. 29 (appellate) and Fed. R. Civ. P. 29 (trial); requires motion or consent of parties.
dataContext: Common in high-profile cases; tracked in public records.
es_definition: Parte no litigante que presenta información para asistir al tribunal sobre un punto de ley o hecho relevante para el caso.
es_context: Comúnmente utilizado en apelaciones; grupos de interés público, organizaciones comerciales y gobiernos presentan amicus briefs.

definition: The monetary value of relief sought in diversity cases; must exceed $75,000 to establish federal diversity jurisdiction.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 1332(a) requires amount in controversy; calculated by looking to plaintiff's complaint allegations.
dataContext: Amount in controversy determines federal court jurisdictional eligibility.
es_definition: Cantidad de dinero en disputa en un caso, utilizada para determinar si existe jurisdicción federal de diversidad.
es_context: Debe exceder $75,000 para la jurisdicción federal de diversidad bajo 28 U.S.C. § 1332.

definition: The defendant's formal response to a complaint, admitting or denying the allegations and raising any affirmative defenses.
federalContext: Must be filed within 21 days under Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(a); failure to respond may result in a default judgment.
dataContext: Tracked as an early responsive pleading in case progression data.
es_definition: Respuesta pleiteada por el demandado a la demanda del demandante, admitiendo o negando cada alegación.
es_context: Debe presentarse dentro de 21 días de ser notificado de la demanda bajo la Regla Federal de Procedimiento Civil 12(a).

definition: A repudiation of contract obligations before the time for performance arrives, allowing the non-breaching party to immediately seek remedies.
federalContext: Recognized in federal courts under applicable state law; Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 250.
dataContext: Allows immediate action in contract disputes without waiting for performance date.
es_definition: Incumplimiento que ocurre cuando una de las partes indica antes del vencimiento que no cumplirá sus obligaciones contractuales.
es_context: Permite a la otra parte rescindir el contrato y buscar remedios de inmediato sin esperar al incumplimiento real.

definition: Administrative Procedure Act; federal statute governing administrative agency procedures, rulemaking, and judicial review.
federalContext: 5 U.S.C. §§ 551-559 establish procedural requirements for federal agencies; § 706 provides judicial review standard.
dataContext: APA governs federal agency litigation and administrative law cases.
es_definition: Ley de Procedimiento Administrativo que establece requisitos procedimentales para agencias federales.
es_context: 5 U.S.C. § 551 et seq.; regula la creación de regulaciones, audiencias administrativas y revisión judicial de acciones de agencias.

definition: A request to a higher court to review and reverse or modify a lower court's judgment.
federalContext: Fed. R. App. P. govern appeals to the Circuit Courts; notice of appeal must be filed within 30 days under Rule 4.
dataContext: Appeal rates and reversal rates indicate trial court decision quality and litigation stakes.
es_definition: Solicitud a un tribunal superior para revisar y posiblemente anular la decisión de un tribunal inferior.
es_context: Las apelaciones federales se presentan ante los Tribunales de Apelaciones del Circuito Unido; limitadas a cuestiones de ley, no de hecho.

definition: A written legal argument submitted to an appellate court setting forth facts, law, and arguments on appeal issues.
federalContext: Fed. R. App. P. 28 governs brief formatting; appellant brief, appellee brief, and reply brief structure appellate briefing.
dataContext: Appellate briefs present appeal arguments.
es_definition: Documento escrito presentado ante un tribunal de apelaciones exponiendo los argumentos legales de una de las partes.
es_context: Debe cumplir con estrictos requisitos de formato y extensión bajo las Reglas de Procedimiento de Apelación del Circuito.

definition: A court with jurisdiction to review decisions of lower courts; includes circuit courts and Supreme Court.
federalContext: Appellate courts review for error; may reverse, affirm, or remand; establish precedent through published opinions.
dataContext: Appellate court filings and opinions are case resolution data.
es_definition: Tribunal con autoridad para revisar decisiones de tribunales inferiores sobre cuestiones de ley.
es_context: Los Tribunales de Apelaciones Federales Unido revisan decisiones de los Tribunales de Distrito Federal en el segundo nivel de revisión.

definition: A standard of judicial review under the Administrative Procedure Act requiring that agency decisions be based on reasoned explanation and consideration of relevant factors.
federalContext: Governed by 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A); applies to most informal agency actions; requires agency explanation.
dataContext: Administrative law standard of review.
es_definition: Estándar de revisión judicial de acciones de agencias federales; la agencia debe explicar racionalmente su decisión.
es_context: La Ley de Procedimiento Administrativo requiere que los tribunales anulen acciones de agencias que sean arbitrarias y caprichosas.

definition: Binding alternative dispute resolution where neutral arbitrator(s) hear evidence and issue final decision enforceable as judgment.
federalContext: Enforced under FAA § 2; arbitrator decision final with limited appeal rights; excludes federal court review.
dataContext: Arbitration clauses eliminate federal court filings.
es_definition: Proceso alternativo de resolución de disputas en el que un tercero neutral, un árbitro, escucha a ambas partes y dicta una decisión vinculante.
es_context: Ampliamente utilizado en disputas contractuales y laborales; puede obligarse mediante cláusulas de arbitraje en acuerdos.

definition: A contract provision requiring parties to resolve disputes through arbitration rather than litigation.
federalContext: Enforceable under FAA § 2; courts must enforce arbitration agreements; arbitration shields parties from federal court jurisdiction.
dataContext: Arbitration clauses eliminate federal court cases; common in commercial and employment contexts.
es_definition: Disposición en un contrato que requiere que las partes resuelvan disputas mediante arbitraje en lugar de litigios.
es_context: Generalmente ejecutable bajo la Ley de Arbitraje Federal (FAA), 9 U.S.C. § 1 et seq.

definition: The constitutional article establishing the federal judiciary and prescribing the jurisdiction, structure, and operation of federal courts.
federalContext: Article III judges have lifetime tenure and salary protection; governs federal question and diversity jurisdiction.
dataContext: Article III authority determines federal court jurisdiction.
es_definition: Sección de la Constitución de EE.UU. que establece la rama judicial federal y define el alcance del poder judicial federal.
es_context: Establece la Corte Suprema, autoriza al Congreso a crear tribunales inferiores y delimita la jurisdicción federal.

definition: A federal judge appointed under Article III with lifetime tenure and salary protection; requires Senate confirmation.
federalContext: District judges and circuit judges are Article III judges; appointed by President with Senate confirmation.
dataContext: Article III judges preside over federal court litigation.
es_definition: Juez federal cuyo cargo está protegido por el Artículo III de la Constitución, tenencia de por vida a buen comportamiento.
es_context: Los Jueces de Distrito Federal y los Jueces de Circuito son Jueces del Artículo III; tienen mayor autoridad que los jueces administrativos.

definition: A defense to negligence where plaintiff knew of risk and voluntarily assumed it, thereby forfeiting right to recover.
federalContext: Express assumption: written agreement; implied assumption: conduct showing assumption; not recognized in some jurisdictions.
dataContext: Assumption of risk defense bars recovery in specific contexts.
es_definition: Defensa que sostiene que el demandante conocía y voluntariamente asumió los riesgos que causaron su lesión.
es_context: Defensa afirmativa comúnmente utilizada en casos de lesiones personales que implican actividades recreativas o riesgosas.

definition: Compensation awarded to prevailing party for reasonable attorney costs; normally recoverable only where statute or contract authorizes.
federalContext: Fee-shifting statutes include Civil Rights Act, Copyright Act, and others; prevailing party analysis complex.
dataContext: Attorney fee awards are significant damage component.
es_definition: Cargos cobrados por un abogado por sus servicios, que pueden ser pagados por el cliente o en algunos casos por la parte vencida.
es_context: En algunos casos estatutarios y en procedimientos de valores, la parte vencida puede ser ordenada a pagar los honorarios del abogado del ganador.

definition: A property condition that poses a hidden risk to children who may be attracted to enter the property, making the property owner liable for injuries even though the children are technically trespassers.
federalContext: Governed by Restatement (Second) of Torts § 339; federal courts apply state law on this doctrine.
dataContext: Product liability and premises liability cases tracked with NOS code 310.
es_definition: Doctrina que sostiene que un propietario es responsable por las lesiones a los intrusos si el propietario conocía el peligro y esperaba intrusión.
es_context: Comúnmente aplicada cuando hay piscinas, equipos de construcción u otros peligros atractivos para los niños en la propiedad.

definition: A doctrine giving deference to an agency's interpretation of its own ambiguous regulations, established in Auer v. Robbins, 519 U.S. 452 (1997).
federalContext: Governed by Auer v. Robbins, 519 U.S. 452 (1997); narrowed by recent Supreme Court decisions; applies to agency interpretations.
dataContext: Administrative law standard of review.
es_definition: Estándar de revisión judicial que requiere deferencia a las interpretaciones de una agencia de su propio reglamento.
es_context: Establece que si un reglamento es ambiguo, las interpretaciones del regulador merecen respeto si son razonables.

definition: Evidence that a matter is genuine; the process of establishing that evidence is what it purports to be.
federalContext: Fed. R. Evid. 901-903 govern authentication; requires showing that evidence is what proponent claims.
dataContext: Authentication disputes arise with documents, digital evidence, and physical exhibits.
es_definition: Proceso de establecer que la evidencia presentada en juicio es genuina y es lo que se afirma que es.
es_context: Regla Federal de Evidencia 901 requiere que la evidencia esté identificada o autenticada suficientemente antes de su admisión.

definition: The frequency or prevalence of an event or characteristic in a population, used in statistical analysis of discrimination.
federalContext: Critical in disparate impact analysis; establishes expected rate; deviation from base rate indicates discrimination.
dataContext: Statistical evidence methodology.
es_definition: La frecuencia general o probabilidad de un evento en toda una población, utilizada en evidencia estadística.
es_context: Importante en casos que involucran análisis estadístico de discriminación o patrones de negligencia.

definition: A trial before a judge without jury; judge determines both facts and law.
federalContext: Parties may waive jury trial by consent and agreement; judge acts as fact-finder and law-applier.
dataContext: Bench trials are less common than jury trials in federal civil cases.
es_definition: Juicio en el que el juez, en lugar de un jurado, decide los hechos y aplica la ley para dictar sentencia.
es_context: Las partes pueden consentir en un juicio ante el tribunal en lugar de un juicio con jurado bajo la Regla Federal de Procedimiento Civil 52.

definition: An evidentiary principle requiring that the original or a duplicate of a document be produced when the terms of a document are at issue in litigation.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Evid. 1001-1008; applies to writings, recordings, and photographs when content is in issue.
dataContext: Key admissibility issue in document-intensive cases.
es_definition: Regla de evidencia que requiere que el original de un documento sea presentado cuando su contenido está en cuestión.
es_context: Regla Federal de Evidencia 1002; permite excepciones cuando el original se ha perdido o destruido sin negligencia.

definition: Separation of a lawsuit into two or more phases of trial (e.g., liability phase and damages phase), to be tried separately.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 42; common in patent litigation and high-stakes cases; judicial discretion.
dataContext: Trial structure decision; affects litigation timeline.
es_definition: División de un juicio en fases separadas, típicamente separando responsabilidad y daños.
es_context: Permite al tribunal resolver primero responsabilidad; si el demandante gana, luego se realiza un juicio separado sobre daños.

definition: A written statement of costs incurred by a party in prosecuting or defending a lawsuit, filed after judgment for potential award against opposing party.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 54(d); identifies taxable costs; subject to objection and taxation by clerk.
dataContext: Cost recovery mechanism; post-judgment filing.
es_definition: Declaración presentada por la parte vencedora detallando los costos de la corte permitidos incurridos en litigio.
es_context: Los costos permitidos incluyen cuotas de presentación y honorarios de testigos peritos; los honorarios de abogados generalmente se excluyen a menos que se otorgue estatutariamente.

definition: Legal authority that courts must follow; includes prior decisions in same jurisdiction and applicable statutes.
federalContext: Supreme Court decisions bind all federal courts; circuit decisions bind district courts in that circuit.
dataContext: Binding authority determines applicable law.
es_definition: Precedente legal que un tribunal debe seguir, típicamente decisiones de tribunales superiores en su jurisdicción.
es_context: Las decisiones de la Corte Suprema son autoridad vinculante para todos los tribunales inferiores; las decisiones de circuito vinculan a los tribunales de distrito.

definition: A private civil action for damages against federal officials for constitutional violations; created by Bivens v. Six Unknown Federal Narcotics Agents.
federalContext: Allows damages against federal officials for constitutional violations; qualified immunity defense available.
dataContext: Bivens actions are less common than Section 1983 suits against state officials.
es_definition: Acción federal contra funcionarios federales por violaciones de derechos constitucionales.
es_context: Establecida en Bivens v. Six Unknown Federal Narcotics Agents; permite demandas por daños contra agentes federales por violaciones de derechos.

definition: Latin for 'in good faith'; acting honestly and fairly without deception or fraud.
federalContext: Used throughout federal law; contrasts with mala fide; establishes good faith standard in many contexts.
dataContext: Standard requirement in contracts, negotiations, and conduct.
es_definition: Actuación honesta y de buena fe; el cumplimiento de las obligaciones legales de manera honesta.
es_context: Defensa en casos de discriminación; un empleador puede discriminar si la característica es una calificación ocupacional de buena fe.

definition: A genuine job-related requirement that permits otherwise prohibited discrimination when occupational qualification is essential.
federalContext: Title VII exception at 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(e); rare; employer must prove qualification reasonably necessary to job performance.
dataContext: BFOQ defense rarely succeeds in discrimination litigation.
es_definition: Excepción a las leyes de igualdad de oportunidades de empleo que permite discriminación basada en una característica protegida si es esencial para el trabajo.
es_context: Ejemplo: género en roles de seguridad donde se requiere una búsqueda de cuerpo del mismo sexo.

definition: A violation of a duty or obligation; in tort law, failure to meet the standard of reasonable care.
federalContext: Plaintiff must prove breach by preponderance of evidence; may be proven through negligence, violation of statute, or custom.
dataContext: Breach findings determine tort liability.
es_definition: Violación de un derecho o deber legal, especialmente el incumplimiento de los términos de un contrato.
es_context: El incumplimiento de un contrato es una causa de acción primaria; permite al demandante buscar daños o ejecución específica.

definition: Failure to perform obligations under a contract without legal excuse; foundation for contract damages claims.
federalContext: Federal courts in diversity apply state contract law under Erie doctrine; elements include valid contract and breach.
dataContext: Breach of contract is basis for contract damages recovery.
es_definition: Acción legal que surge del incumplimiento no justificado de los términos de un acuerdo contractual.
es_context: Requiere demostrar que existía un contrato válido, que el demandado incumplió y que el demandante sufrió daños.

definition: A litigation process in which the burden of proof or persuasion moves between parties at different stages based on evidence presented.
federalContext: Applied in discrimination cases, summary judgment, and other contexts; governed by Fed. R. Evid. and case law.
dataContext: Proof standard methodology.
es_definition: Procedimiento legal en el cual el demandante presenta un caso de acción, luego la carga se desplaza al demandado para demostrar una defensa.
es_context: Utilizado en casos de discriminación bajo la prueba de McDonnell Douglas; el demandante establece una presunción de discriminación.

definition: An exception to the hearsay rule allowing records made and kept in the regular course of business to be admitted without the maker's availability to testify.
federalContext: Codified in Fed. R. Evid. 803(6); requires foundation establishing regularity of keeping records.
dataContext: Key exception for documentary evidence in all case types.
es_definition: Excepción a la regla de rumores que permite la admisión de registros comerciales mantenidos en el curso regular del negocio.
es_context: Regla Federal de Evidencia 803(6); requiere que los registros se creen y mantengan en el curso regular del negocio.

definition: An order by a trial judge establishing procedures, schedules, and requirements for a specific case, often following initial conferences.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 16; sets deadlines for discovery, discovery disputes, and trial; controls case progression.
dataContext: Individual case governance; critical scheduling document.
es_definition: Orden emitida por el tribunal durante la conferencia de gestión que establece cronogramas y procedimientos para el litigio.
es_context: Regla Federal de Procedimiento Civil 16; puede incluir plazos de descubrimiento, fechas de juicio y órdenes de confidencialidad.

definition: The causal connection between defendant's conduct and plaintiff's injury, comprising both cause-in-fact and proximate cause.
federalContext: Essential element of tort liability; requires both but-for causation and proximate cause.
dataContext: Causation findings determine tort liability.
es_definition: Conexión entre la conducta de un acusado y la lesión o daño sufrido por el demandante.
es_context: En casos de negligencia, se requiere tanto causa 'pero por' como 'causa proximal'; vital en casos de lesiones corporales.

definition: Latin for 'let the buyer beware'; a principle shifting risk to buyer for defects unless seller made express warranty.
federalContext: Modified by warranties under UCC § 2-314 and consumer protection laws; limited application in modern law.
dataContext: Sales law principle; product liability context.
es_definition: Principio legal que significa 'que el comprador tenga cuidado'; trasplanta el riesgo de calidad al comprador.
es_context: Ampliamente modificado por estatutos de derechos de los consumidores y garantías implícitas en la venta de mercancías.

definition: A writ issued by an appellate court to a lower court, ordering it to send up a case for review.
federalContext: The Supreme Court primarily uses certiorari; Rule 10 of the Supreme Court Rules lists factors considered in granting certiorari petitions.
dataContext: Supreme Court certiorari grants are significant appellate milestones.
es_definition: Orden de una corte superior que requiere que una corte inferior certifique y presente registros de un caso para revisión judicial.
es_context: La Corte Suprema otorga circa 70-80 peticiones de certiorari de aproximadamente 7,000 peticiones presentadas anualmente.

definition: The chronological documentation of evidence handling, showing who possessed it and when, ensuring evidence integrity.
federalContext: Critical for authentication under Fed. R. Evid. 901; breaks in chain may render evidence inadmissible.
dataContext: Chain of custody issues arise in cases involving physical or digital evidence.
es_definition: Documentación que demuestra que la evidencia física fue recopilada, manejada y almacenada de manera que previene alteración o contaminación.
es_context: Crítica en litigios penales y civiles; debilidades pueden resultar en la exclusión de evidencia.

definition: An unlimited challenge to remove a prospective juror based on showing grounds for bias, prejudice, or inability to be impartial.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 47(c); unlimited number; requires showing of actual bias or ground for exclusion.
dataContext: Jury selection tool; contrasts with peremptory challenges.
es_definition: Solicitud del abogado para que el tribunal remueva a un posible jurado que ha demostrado prejuicio o parcialidad.
es_context: A diferencia de los desafíos perentorios, hay un número ilimitado de desafíos por causa si se demuestra causa suficiente.

definition: Evidence of a person's character, traits, or propensities, generally inadmissible in civil cases and limited in criminal cases to prove conduct on a specific occasion.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Evid. 404-405; narrowly constrained; exceptions include habit evidence and prior acts of sexual assault.
dataContext: Rarely admitted in civil litigation; key issue in character evidence motions.
es_definition: Evidencia de la propensión de una persona a actuar de cierta manera, utilizada para inferir conducta consistente.
es_context: Generalmente inadmisible para demostrar conducta conformante excepto en circunstancias específicas bajo la Regla Federal de Evidencia 404.

definition: A doctrine requiring courts to defer to reasonable agency interpretation of ambiguous statutes it administers.
federalContext: Chevron U.S.A., Inc. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc. established two-step test for statutory interpretation.
dataContext: Chevron deference is standard in administrative litigation.
es_definition: Estándar de revisión judicial de interpretaciones de agencias de estatutos que administran, requiriendo deferencia si la ley es ambigua.
es_context: Establecido en Chevron U.S.A., Inc. v. NRDC; permite interpretación de agencia si no contradice clara intención legislativa.

definition: Federal appellate courts reviewing district court decisions; 13 circuits (12 regional plus Federal Circuit); panels typically sit.
federalContext: Circuit courts review district court judgments; en banc review available; decisions establish circuit precedent.
dataContext: Circuit court decisions establish binding circuit precedent.
es_definition: Tribunal de apelaciones federal; hay 13 Tribunales de Apelaciones del Circuito Unido que revisan decisiones de los tribunales de distrito.
es_context: Cada circuito cubre múltiples estados; las apelaciones de los tribunales de distrito van al tribunal de circuito para la segunda revisión.

definition: A court's interpretation of patent claim scope and meaning; determines what accused device must do to infringe.
federalContext: Markman hearing determines claim scope; intrinsic evidence (claims, specification) preferred over extrinsic evidence.
dataContext: Claim construction is preliminary patent infringement issue.
es_definition: En litigios de patentes, el proceso de interpretar el significado y alcance de las reclamaciones de patente.
es_context: Decisión previa a la prueba en casos de patentes; determina el alcance de protección de la patente y es frecuentemente el tema de apelación.

definition: A lawsuit filed by a representative on behalf of a group of persons with common claims or defenses.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 23 requires numerosity, commonality, typicality, and adequate representation; must satisfy Rule 23(b) requirements.
dataContext: Class certification dates and class size are significant case milestones affecting damages exposure.

definition: A doctrine preventing re-litigation of an issue decided in a prior case between the same parties.
federalContext: Federal courts apply issue preclusion when: (1) issue identical to prior case, (2) actually litigated, (3) decided in prior judgment.
dataContext: Collateral estoppel defenses reduce discovery and trial scope.

definition: A rule preventing reduction of damages based on payments from collateral sources (insurance, welfare, gifts) not traceable to the defendant.
federalContext: Applied in most jurisdictions; protects plaintiff from double recovery reduction; has exceptions for certain insurance payments.
dataContext: Damages law principle; affects damage calculation.

definition: A doctrine allocating damages proportionally based on each party's comparative fault; reduces recovery by plaintiff's percentage of fault.
federalContext: Applied in federal courts; most states use comparative negligence; may bar recovery if plaintiff is more negligent than defendant.
dataContext: Comparative negligence findings reduce damage awards proportionally.

definition: Damages awarded to compensate plaintiff for actual loss or harm, including economic and non-economic damages.
federalContext: Standard remedy for tort and breach of contract; must be reasonably foreseeable and proven with reasonable certainty.
dataContext: Compensatory damages make up majority of damage awards.

definition: A formal written accusation or assertion that initiates a civil lawsuit, setting forth the facts and legal basis for the plaintiff's claim.
federalContext: In federal court, complaints must comply with Fed. R. Civ. P. 8 and contain sufficient facts to state a claim for relief under Rule 12(b)(6).
dataContext: Initial pleadings tracked in FJIC with filing codes indicating case nature.

definition: A range of values around a sample statistic that is likely to contain the true population parameter with a specified probability.
federalContext: Used in statistical evidence; 95% confidence interval standard in many fields; Daubert analysis applies.
dataContext: Statistical evidence methodology.

definition: A court-approved settlement agreement in which parties agree to be bound by specific terms and a court retains jurisdiction to enforce compliance.
federalContext: Requires court approval; parties waive right to contest liability; court retains enforcement jurisdiction; common in employment and civil rights cases.
dataContext: Settlement mechanism; binding court order.

definition: Something of value exchanged by parties to a contract; required element establishing mutual assent and binding obligation.
federalContext: Essential to valid contract; may be monetary or non-monetary; adequacy not typically questioned by courts.
dataContext: Consideration disputes affect contract validity.

definition: A court order combining separate lawsuits involving common questions of fact or law into a single action for efficiency.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 42(a); court discretion; promotes judicial economy but may affect individual fairness.
dataContext: Multi-docket coordination; case management decision.

definition: An employee's forced resignation due to intolerable working conditions created or known by employer, treated as an involuntary termination.
federalContext: Recognized in federal courts; requires showing conditions would cause reasonable person to resign; plaintiff must actually resign.
dataContext: Constructive discharge claims appear in employment litigation.

definition: Willful violation of court order or disrespect toward court; punishable by fine or imprisonment.
federalContext: Civil contempt: coercive sanction to compel compliance; criminal contempt: punitive sanction for deliberate violation.
dataContext: Contempt findings indicate non-compliance with court orders.

definition: A fee agreement where attorney is paid a percentage of recovery only if case succeeds; aligns attorney and client interests.
federalContext: Permitted in most civil cases; prohibited in domestic relations; subject to reasonableness requirement.
dataContext: Contingency fee arrangements affect litigation incentives.

definition: A patent application filed by an applicant claiming benefit of an earlier application, with same specification but different or broader claims.
federalContext: Governed by 35 U.S.C. § 120; preserves original filing date; used to obtain additional claims or pursue different aspects.
dataContext: Patent prosecution strategy; claim scope expansion.

definition: The right of a defendant to recover from other jointly liable defendants their proportionate share of damages awarded against all defendants.
federalContext: Governed by state law in most jurisdictions; related to joint and several liability; allows shifting of burden among defendants.
dataContext: Multi-defendant settlement and judgment allocation.

definition: Plaintiff's own negligence that contributed to injury; in pure comparative negligence jurisdictions, reduces recovery proportionally.
federalContext: Pure comparative negligence: plaintiff may recover even if more negligent; several states use bar rule.
dataContext: Contributory negligence findings affect damage awards.

definition: Exclusive right to reproduce, distribute, perform, or display original work of authorship; protects creative works.
federalContext: 17 U.S.C. governs copyright; federal question jurisdiction; infringement requires copying and substantial similarity.
dataContext: Copyright cases involve creative works and digital content.

definition: A claim by a defendant against a plaintiff, either compulsory (arising from same transaction) or permissive (involving different transaction).
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 13; compulsory counterclaims must be asserted or are waived.
dataContext: Common in commercial disputes; expands case scope.

definition: A patent claiming a method or apparatus for performing data processing or other operations related to financial management, now subject to post-grant review under transitional rules.
federalContext: Governed by 35 U.S.C. § 321-329; transitional proceeding; broader grounds than IPR; limited to 2011-2019.
dataContext: Software/business method patent validity challenges.

definition: An appeal filed by the appellee raising additional issues for appellate review of the trial court judgment.
federalContext: Fed. R. App. P. 28.1 governs cross-appeals; permits appellee to challenge parts of judgment favorable to appellant.
dataContext: Cross-appeals appear in appellate litigation.

definition: A claim by one defendant against another defendant, or by one plaintiff against another plaintiff, arising from the same transaction or occurrence.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 13(g); does not require leave of court; must relate to same transaction/occurrence.
dataContext: Multi-party claims tracked in case administration.

definition: Monetary compensation awarded by a court to compensate a plaintiff for loss, injury, or harm.
federalContext: May be compensatory (actual damages), punitive (punishment), or nominal (minimal amount); jury assesses damages within award ranges.
dataContext: Damage awards are primary case value metric.

definition: A challenge to the admissibility of expert witness testimony based on the reliability of scientific methodology and principles.
federalContext: Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals established factors for assessing expert testimony; Fed. R. Evid. 702 codifies Daubert reliability standard.
dataContext: Daubert motions are common in technical and pharmaceutical cases.

definition: Latin for 'in fact'; occurring in reality, regardless of legal status, as distinguished from de jure.
federalContext: Used to describe unofficial or unlawful conditions; contrasts with de jure; relevant in constitutional and administrative law.
dataContext: Factual status description.

definition: Latin for 'by law'; formally or legally, regardless of actual facts.
federalContext: Used to describe official or lawful status; contrasts with de facto; relevant in all areas of law.
dataContext: Legal status description.

definition: A standard of judicial review in which the court reviews an issue without deference to the lower court or agency's decision.
federalContext: Applied to questions of law; questions of fact reviewed for clear error; appellate standard varies by context.
dataContext: Appellate review standard.

definition: A written statement made under penalty of perjury, equivalent to an affidavit but not requiring a notary.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 1746 permits declarations in federal court under penalty of perjury as alternative to affidavits.
dataContext: Declarations are widely used in federal litigation for efficiency.

definition: A judgment by a court stating the legal rights of parties or the legal status of a matter without ordering specific relief.
federalContext: Governed by 28 U.S.C. § 2201-2202 and Fed. R. Civ. P. 57; requires actual case or controversy; appropriate when plaintiff seeks clarification.
dataContext: Distinct remedy form; clarifies legal status.

definition: A judgment rendered against a defendant who fails to answer or appear in court, resulting in automatic loss of the case.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 55 governs default judgments; clerk may enter default, but judge enters judgment.
dataContext: Default judgments indicate defendant non-engagement; common in debt collection cases.

definition: A prison official's conscious disregard for inmate health or safety; required element in Section 1983 prison conditions claims.
federalContext: Established in Estelle v. Gamble; plaintiff must show serious medical need and deliberate indifference by official.
dataContext: Deliberate indifference claims appear in prisoner litigation.

definition: A pleading asserting that even if the opposing party's allegations are true, they are insufficient in law to support a claim or defense (largely replaced by motion to dismiss under Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6)).
federalContext: Superseded by Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6) motion in federal practice; still used in some state courts.
dataContext: Historical pleading practice; modern federal courts use Rule 12(b)(6).

definition: A sworn oral statement made by a witness or party before a court officer, typically recorded and transcribed for use in litigation.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 30-31; depositions may be used at trial to impeach or corroborate testimony.
dataContext: Number of depositions often correlates with litigation cost and case severity.

definition: A patent protecting the ornamental design or appearance of an article of manufacture, separate from utility patents.
federalContext: Governed by 35 U.S.C. § 171-173; 15-year term from grant date; infringement analyzed by ordinary observer test.
dataContext: Intellectual property protection; tracked in patent litigation.

definition: A judgment granted by a trial judge when the evidence presented is so compelling that a reasonable jury must find for the moving party.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 50 governs judgment as a matter of law (JMOL); post-trial JMOL can overturn jury verdict.
dataContext: Rare in federal practice; indicates one-sided evidentiary presentation.

definition: The pre-trial process by which parties exchange information, documents, and evidence relevant to their claims and defenses.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 26-37; includes interrogatories, requests for production, depositions, and requests for admission.
dataContext: Discovery disputes and motion filings indicate case complexity and litigation intensity.

definition: Unequal or differential treatment of individuals or groups based on protected characteristics such as race, color, religion, or national origin.
federalContext: Title VII (42 U.S.C. § 2000e) prohibits employment discrimination; civil rights laws provide private right of action for discrimination.
dataContext: Discrimination claims appear in employment and civil rights NOS codes.

definition: Discrimination occurring when a facially neutral employment practice has a disproportionate adverse effect on a protected class.
federalContext: Established in Griggs v. Duke Power Co.; requires evidence of neutral policy with disparate effect; employer may defend with business necessity.
dataContext: Disparate impact claims often involve hiring, promotion, or compensation policies.

definition: Discrimination based on intentional differential treatment of similarly-situated individuals; requires proof of discriminatory intent.
federalContext: Established in McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green; plaintiff must show: (1) protected class, (2) qualification, (3) adverse employment action, (4) similarly-situated employee treated better.
dataContext: Disparate treatment is the most common employment discrimination theory.

definition: The manner in which a case is resolved; categories include judgment on verdict, summary judgment, settlement, dismissal, default.
federalContext: FJIC tracks case disposition; disposition type affects case outcome analysis and statistics.
dataContext: Disposition codes track case resolution methods.

definition: The trial-level courts of the federal judiciary; 95 federal district courts with jurisdiction over federal question and diversity cases.
federalContext: Each district has one or more judges; district courts have original jurisdiction over federal and diversity cases.
dataContext: District court filings are primary federal case data source.

definition: Federal court jurisdiction based on complete diversity of citizenship between parties and amount in controversy exceeding $75,000.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 1332 grants diversity jurisdiction; requires complete diversity and exceeding amount in controversy threshold.
dataContext: Diversity cases form significant portion of federal docket.

definition: A patent application filed to claim benefit of an earlier application, dividing the claims to overcome an examiner's rejection based on lack of unity of invention.
federalContext: Governed by 35 U.S.C. § 121; preserves original filing date; used when examiner objects to multiple inventions in one application.
dataContext: Patent prosecution strategy; addresses examiner objections.

definition: A doctrine allowing finding of patent infringement when a product performs substantially the same function in substantially the same way to achieve substantially the same result as the patented invention.
federalContext: Established in Warner-Jenkinson Co. v. Hilton Davis Chem. Co., 520 U.S. 17 (1997); subject to prosecution history estoppel.
dataContext: Patent infringement analysis; broader than literal infringement.
source: Warner-Jenkinson Co. v. Hilton Davis Chem. Co., 520 U.S. 17 (1997)

definition: A legal obligation to exercise reasonable care to avoid harm to others; established by law and specific circumstances.
federalContext: Standard duty owed to foreseeable plaintiffs; exceptions for public officials (qualified immunity) and specific relationships.
dataContext: Duty of care findings are foundational negligence issue.

definition: An exception to the hearsay rule allowing statements made by a person believing death is imminent about the cause or circumstances of what the person believes will cause their death.
federalContext: Codified in Fed. R. Evid. 804(b)(2); requires declarant unavailable and belief in impending death.
dataContext: Limited exception; strict requirements for admissibility in cases involving death.

definition: Monetary compensation for measurable financial losses including lost wages, medical expenses, property damage, and lost earning capacity.
federalContext: Awarded in all damage cases; calculated with evidence and expert testimony; includes present and future losses.
dataContext: Damages calculation component; typically largest portion of damages.

definition: A doctrine holding that a defendant must take the plaintiff as they find them, meaning damages may be recovered for all injuries resulting from the defendant's negligence even if the plaintiff had pre-existing conditions that made injuries more severe.
federalContext: Applied in federal courts under state tort law; establishes that a defendant cannot use plaintiff's fragility as a defense to limit damages.
dataContext: Important in medical malpractice and product liability cases involving vulnerable plaintiffs.

definition: A hearing by all judges of a court rather than a panel; used in appellate courts for reconsideration of panel decisions.
federalContext: Circuit courts may sit en banc for reconsideration of panel decisions; requires vote of majority of active judges.
dataContext: En banc decisions are rare and highly significant.

definition: The formal recording of judgment in the docket; creates finality and begins appeal period.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 58 requires formal entry of judgment; notice of entry triggers 30-day appeal period under Rule 4.
dataContext: Judgment entry dates mark case resolution.

definition: Federal statute prohibiting discrimination in compensation for men and women who perform substantially equal work, with four affirmative defenses.
federalContext: 29 U.S.C. § 206(d); enforced by EEOC; private right of action; damages include back pay and liquidated damages.
dataContext: Employment compensation discrimination; federal question jurisdiction.

definition: Remedies other than monetary damages, granted at the discretion of a court of equity, including injunctions, specific performance, and restitution.
federalContext: Available in federal courts for violations of federal law; subject to equitable doctrines and discretion; no jury trial.
dataContext: Non-monetary remedy; distinct from damages.

definition: The principle that federal courts sitting in diversity must apply state substantive law and federal procedural law.
federalContext: Erie R.R. Co. v. Tompkins established doctrine; federal courts apply state law to substantive issues, Federal Rules to procedural ones.
dataContext: Erie doctrine affects choice of law in diversity cases.

definition: Latin for 'by one party'; a proceeding, hearing, or order made for or by one party without the other party being heard.
federalContext: Generally disfavored; required only in emergency situations; opposing party gets notice and right to be heard.
dataContext: Procedural mechanism; limited use in federal practice.

definition: Law enforcement use of force beyond that reasonably necessary to effectuate a lawful arrest or purpose; constitutional violation.
federalContext: Fourth Amendment claim under Section 1983; objective reasonableness test applied; significant damages award cases.
dataContext: Excessive force claims appear in police liability litigation.

definition: An exception to the hearsay rule allowing statements made by a person under the stress of excitement caused by a startling event.
federalContext: Codified in Fed. R. Evid. 803(2); requires statement made while declarant was still under stress of the event.
dataContext: Commonly used exception in personal injury and criminal cases.

definition: A doctrine requiring parties to pursue all available administrative review procedures before seeking judicial review of an agency action.
federalContext: Governed by 5 U.S.C. § 704 and common law; exceptions exist for futility, inadequacy, and ripeness; case-specific.
dataContext: Procedural requirement in administrative appeals.

definition: A witness qualified by education, training, or experience to testify on specialized matters beyond common knowledge.
federalContext: Fed. R. Evid. 702 permits expert testimony if reliable; Daubert factors assess reliability of expert methodology.
dataContext: Expert witness designation and Daubert challenges are significant case milestones.

definition: A copyright exception permitting limited use of copyrighted material for purposes like criticism, commentary, news, or education.
federalContext: 17 U.S.C. § 107 permits fair use; four-factor test: purpose, nature, amount, and market effect; affirmative defense.
dataContext: Fair use defenses appear in copyright litigation.

definition: Federal statute imposing civil liability on persons who submit false claims for payment to the United States government, including treble damages and penalties.
federalContext: 31 U.S.C. § 3729 et seq.; enforced by Department of Justice and private relators; qui tam provisions.
dataContext: Government fraud case type; federal question jurisdiction.

definition: Federal court jurisdiction based on claims arising under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 1331 grants federal question jurisdiction over federal law claims; no minimum amount in controversy.
dataContext: Federal question cases comprise majority of federal civil docket.

definition: The procedural rules governing civil litigation in the United States federal courts, covering pleading, discovery, motion practice, trial, and appeals.
federalContext: Adopted under 28 U.S.C. § 2072; apply in all federal district courts; supersede state rules in federal question cases.
dataContext: Core procedural framework for federal civil litigation.

definition: A statutory provision making loser pay winner's attorney fees; shifts litigation costs between parties.
federalContext: Available under specific statutes like Civil Rights Act § 1988, Equal Access to Justice Act, and others.
dataContext: Fee-shifting statutes increase litigation stakes.

definition: A decision by a federal agency that is binding, has operative effect, and is not subject to further review within the agency.
federalContext: Governed by 5 U.S.C. § 704; prerequisite for federal judicial review under APA; establishes reviewability.
dataContext: Administrative law litigation; jurisdictional requirement.

definition: A judgment resolving all claims on the merits; creates finality and preclusive effect under res judicata.
federalContext: Appealable under 28 U.S.C. § 1291; creates res judicata and collateral estoppel effects.
dataContext: Final judgments establish case outcomes.

definition: A court order issued before trial memorializing the issues to be tried, exhibits, witnesses, and other trial preparation matters.
federalContext: Issued following pretrial conference; supersedes pleadings; controls trial scope; controls what can be introduced.
dataContext: Trial governance document; limits surprise.

definition: Federal statute requiring covered employers to provide eligible employees up to 12 weeks of unpaid leave per year for qualifying family or medical reasons.
federalContext: 29 U.S.C. § 2601 et seq.; enforced by Department of Labor; private right of action; damages for lost wages and benefits.
dataContext: Employment benefits case type; federal question jurisdiction.

definition: Freedom of Information Act; federal statute providing public access to federal agency records subject to exemptions.
federalContext: 5 U.S.C. § 552 governs FOIA; agencies must release requested documents unless exempt; judicial review available.
dataContext: FOIA litigation appears in administrative docket.

definition: A clause excusing performance when unforeseen events beyond parties' control make performance impossible.
federalContext: Interpreted narrowly; must identify specific events or categories; does not excuse pre-existing contractual obligations.
dataContext: Force majeure claims appear in contract interpretation disputes.

definition: The predicate showing that a witness has personal knowledge and that evidence is authentic before it may be admitted.
federalContext: Fed. R. Evid. 602 requires witness personal knowledge; foundation questioning establishes witness competency and evidence authenticity.
dataContext: Foundation objections are routine in trial transcripts.

definition: A defense to breach of contract when a supervening event completely destroys the basic reason for the contract, making performance pointless, though performance remains physically possible.
federalContext: Recognized in federal courts under applicable state law; Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 265; requires very high threshold.
dataContext: Narrower doctrine than impossibility; applies when purpose, not performance, is defeated.

definition: A settlement agreement allowing a settling defendant to be released but preserving the plaintiff's right to pursue non-settling defendants.
federalContext: Contractual settlement mechanism; defendant released for set amount; plaintiff can recover more from others; limits joint liability.
dataContext: Settlement mechanism in multi-defendant cases.

definition: Damages for non-economic losses such as pain and suffering, mental anguish, and loss of enjoyment of life.
federalContext: No fixed formula; jury determines based on severity and duration; subject to jury instructions.
dataContext: General damages awards vary significantly by case.

definition: A legal action through which a person can seek relief from unlawful detention or imprisonment.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 2254 governs habeas corpus petitions by state prisoners; § 2255 applies to federal prisoners; challenges legality of imprisonment.
dataContext: Habeas corpus cases appear under NOS codes 530, 535, and 510.

definition: Evidence of a person's regular response to specific circumstances, admissible to prove the person acted consistently with the habit on a particular occasion.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Evid. 406; admissible to show action in conformity with the habit; narrower than character evidence.
dataContext: Used in cases involving routine conduct or business practices.

definition: An out-of-court statement offered to prove the truth of the matter asserted, generally inadmissible unless an exception applies.
federalContext: Fed. R. Evid. 801-807 define and govern hearsay; numerous exceptions exist including statements against interest and dying declarations.
dataContext: Hearsay objections frequently appear in discovery and trial transcripts.

definition: Damages awarded for the loss of pleasure, happiness, and enjoyment of life caused by injury or wrongful death.
federalContext: Non-economic damages; calculated using hedonic damage methodology; admissibility varies by jurisdiction; expert testimony often required.
dataContext: Damages component; valuation of life quality loss.

definition: A settlement agreement establishing minimum (low) and maximum (high) amounts that a defendant will pay, regardless of jury verdict.
federalContext: Contractual settlement mechanism; must be disclosed to jury if part of jury verdict; popular in high-stakes litigation.
dataContext: Settlement structuring mechanism.

definition: Severe or pervasive unwelcome harassment or conduct based on protected status, altering terms and conditions of employment.
federalContext: Title VII standard established in Meritor Savings Bank v. Vinson; employer liable unless took reasonable steps to prevent harassment.
dataContext: Hostile work environment claims are common in sexual harassment and discrimination cases.

definition: Evidence or testimony that attacks the credibility of a witness, showing the witness is untrustworthy or has made contradictory statements.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Evid. 607, 613, and other rules; includes prior inconsistent statements, bias, and convictions.
dataContext: Cross-examination tool to undermine witness testimony.

definition: A procedure allowing a defendant to bring a third party (who may be liable to the defendant for all or part of the plaintiff's claim) into the lawsuit.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 14; defendant initiates by filing third-party complaint; adds parties and claims.
dataContext: Expands party composition; tracked in multi-party litigation.

definition: A defense to breach of contract based on subsequent events that make contract performance objectively impossible, excusing performance and discharging the contract.
federalContext: Recognized in federal courts under applicable state law; Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 262.
dataContext: Force majeure related doctrine; key in pandemic and disaster litigation.

definition: Latin for 'in chambers'; a proceeding conducted privately by a judge in the judge's office, not in open court.
federalContext: Used for sensitive matters, settlement discussions, and privilege issues; ensures privacy; decisions often confidential.
dataContext: Private judicial proceeding.

definition: Latin for 'at the threshold'; a motion or order made before trial to determine admissibility of evidence before it is offered.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Evid. and Fed. R. Civ. P. 16; determines what evidence can be presented to jury; critical trial preparation.
dataContext: Pre-trial motion; evidence admissibility determination.

definition: An obligation to compensate another for loss, damage, or liability; contractual allocation of risk between parties.
federalContext: Common in commercial and construction contracts; indemnitors agree to defend and hold harmless indemnitees.
dataContext: Indemnification disputes arise in multi-party litigation.

definition: An agreement or legal obligation requiring one party to compensate another for losses or liability incurred by that party.
federalContext: Governed by state contract law; creates right to recover from indemnitee for losses caused by indemnitee; common in construction and commercial contracts.
dataContext: Risk allocation mechanism in multi-party cases.

definition: A defense to patent infringement based on fraud, misrepresentation, or inequitable conduct by the patentee during prosecution, rendering the patent unenforceable.
federalContext: Established in Therasense, Inc. v. Becton, Dickinson & Co., 649 F.3d 1276 (Fed. Cir. 2011); requires clear and convincing evidence of material misrepresentation.
dataContext: Patent validity defense; patent prosecution issues.
source: Therasense, Inc. v. Becton, Dickinson & Co., 649 F.3d 1276 (Fed. Cir. 2011)

definition: A court order requiring a person to do or refrain from doing a specified act.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 65 governs preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders; requires likelihood of success and irreparable harm.
dataContext: Injunction grants indicate preliminary relief success; common in IP and employment cases.

definition: Latin for 'among other things'; used to indicate that listed items are not exhaustive.
federalContext: Common legal phrase; used throughout legal writing to indicate illustrative examples.
dataContext: Legal writing convention.

definition: A post-grant administrative proceeding before the USPTO Patent Trial and Appeal Board (PTAB) in which a third party challenges patent validity based on prior art.
federalContext: Governed by 35 U.S.C. § 311-319 and 37 C.F.R. § 42; established by America Invents Act; alternative to district court litigation.
dataContext: Patent validity challenges; key proceeding in patent disputes.

definition: An appeal of a non-final order before final judgment; permitted in limited circumstances under 28 U.S.C. § 1292.
federalContext: Available for orders granting/denying injunctions (§ 1292(a)(1)), certain certified orders (§ 1292(b)), and bankruptcy appeals.
dataContext: Interlocutory appeals are rare and affect case timeline.

definition: An appeal of an interlocutory order (an order that does not finally dispose of the case) before entry of a final judgment, available only in limited circumstances.
federalContext: Governed by 28 U.S.C. § 1292; includes orders granting/denying preliminary injunctions and certified class actions.
dataContext: Tracked separately from final appeals; limits appellate jurisdiction.

definition: A civil action allowing a person holding property claimed by multiple parties to deposit the property with a court and have the claimants litigate their rights.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 22 and 28 U.S.C. § 1335; federal interpleader statute provides independent federal question jurisdiction.
dataContext: Special case type in federal practice; common in insurance disputes.

definition: The range between the 25th and 75th percentiles; measures spread of middle 50% of data.
federalContext: Used in case statistics to describe typical damages and settlement ranges.
dataContext: Interquartile range describes settlement value spread.

definition: A written question submitted by one party to another during discovery, which must be answered under oath.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 33 limits interrogatories to 25 per party unless modified by agreement or court order.
dataContext: Number of interrogatories filed indicates discovery scope and litigation intensity.

definition: A procedure allowing a non-party to join litigation as either an intervenor-of-right or permissive intervenor based on interest in the subject matter or claim.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 24; intervenor-of-right requires direct interest; permissive intervention is discretionary.
dataContext: Party joinder tracked in case administration.

definition: Latin for 'by the fact itself'; inherently or necessarily, without need for further proof.
federalContext: Used to indicate automatic legal consequences flowing from facts; illustrative rather than conclusive.
dataContext: Legal analysis convention.

definition: The combining of claims, parties, or actions in a single lawsuit for efficiency and judicial economy.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 13-25; includes joinder of claims, parties, and counterclaims; consolidation under Rule 42.
dataContext: Case type indicator; affects litigation structure.

definition: A legal doctrine holding multiple defendants jointly responsible for a plaintiff's injuries, allowing the plaintiff to recover the full amount of damages from any one defendant.
federalContext: Scope varies by state; federal courts apply state law; some jurisdictions limit this through comparative negligence statutes.
dataContext: Key issue in multi-defendant product liability and tort cases.

definition: A court's official and final decision regarding the rights and liabilities of parties to a lawsuit.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 54-60 govern judgments, including entry, amendment, and relief from judgment.
dataContext: Judgment amounts and dates are primary case resolution data points.

term: Judgment as a Matter of Law (Directed Verdict)
definition: A judgment rendered on the court's order that no reasonable jury could render a different verdict, removing the case from the jury.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 50; motion must be made before or after jury verdict; high standard; rarely granted.
dataContext: Summary disposition of case without jury verdict.

definition: A judgment entered after a jury verdict for one party, reversing or setting aside that verdict based on insufficiency of evidence to support it.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 50(b); motion must follow judgment as matter of law motion; reverses verdict.
dataContext: Post-trial remedy; case disposition indicator.

definition: A judge's acceptance of a fact as established without requiring proof, permissible for adjudicative and legislative facts.
federalContext: Fed. R. Evid. 201 governs judicial notice; may be taken of adjudicative facts not reasonably subject to dispute.
dataContext: Judicial notice expedites proceedings by eliminating proof requirements for undisputed facts.

definition: A court's power to hear and decide a case and to enforce its judgment.
federalContext: Federal courts have subject matter jurisdiction over federal questions (28 U.S.C. § 1331) and diversity cases (28 U.S.C. § 1332).
dataContext: Jurisdiction type (federal question vs. diversity) affects applicable law and appeal rates.

definition: A group of citizens sworn to hear evidence and determine facts in a civil or criminal case; right to jury protected by Seventh Amendment.
federalContext: Civil jury typically twelve in federal court; verdicts must be unanimous; jury deliberates facts under judge's legal instructions.
dataContext: Jury trial rates vary by case type and district.

definition: A judge's oral or written explanation of the law applicable to the case that jurors must follow when deciding their verdict.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 51 governs jury instructions; Federal Jury Practice and Instructions (5th ed.) provides model instructions.
dataContext: Jury instruction disputes often lead to appeals on grounds of erroneous instructions.

definition: A doctrine allowing a plaintiff to recover in negligence despite comparative negligence, where the defendant had the final opportunity to avoid the injury but failed to exercise reasonable care.
federalContext: Largely superseded by comparative negligence statutes; still recognized in some jurisdictions.
dataContext: Historical importance in negligence cases; less common in modern litigation.

definition: A witness who testifies based on personal observation rather than specialized expertise or training.
federalContext: Fed. R. Evid. 701 permits lay opinion on matters within personal knowledge; opinions on legal conclusions prohibited.
dataContext: Lay witnesses are standard in most trials.

definition: A legal claim on property securing payment of a debt or obligation; creditor's right to retain possession until debt paid.
federalContext: Federal tax liens, judgment liens, and medical provider liens commonly appear in personal injury settlements.
dataContext: Liens reduce net settlement proceeds.

definition: Pre-agreed damages amount specified in contract to compensate for breach; must be reasonable estimate of anticipated harm.
federalContext: Distinguished from penalty clauses; enforceable if reasonable forecast of anticipated harm; unenforceable if penalty.
dataContext: Liquidated damages clauses determine breach remedies.

definition: Procedural rules adopted by individual federal district courts or circuits to supplement the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and address local court practices.
federalContext: Adopted under Fed. R. Civ. P. 83; must comply with Federal Rules; vary by court; critical for litigation planning.
dataContext: Court-specific procedural requirements.

definition: Damages recoverable by spouse or family member for deprivation of companionship, affection, and services due to injury or death.
federalContext: Available in tort cases when defendant's negligence injures plaintiff; varies by state on categories of claimants.
dataContext: Loss of consortium damages appear in personal injury cases.

definition: A single payment of the entire settlement or judgment amount rather than periodic payments.
federalContext: Traditional settlement method; plaintiff receives single payment on settlement date.
dataContext: Lump sum settlements indicate risk allocation between parties.

definition: A federal judicial officer with limited jurisdiction over minor criminal offenses and civil matters up to specified monetary limits.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 631 establishes magistrate judges; appointed by district courts; preside over misdemeanors and civil claims under $100,000.
dataContext: Magistrate judge cases represent significant federal docket volume.

definition: Latin for 'in bad faith'; acting dishonestly or fraudulently with intent to deceive.
federalContext: Contrasts with bona fide; establishes bad faith standard; bars relief in many contexts.
dataContext: Standard requirement; grounds for adverse inference.

definition: A writ compelling a court or government official to perform a legal duty they are refusing or neglecting to perform.
federalContext: Governed by 28 U.S.C. § 1651; available in federal court to compel action by lower court judges or federal officials.
dataContext: Mandamus petitions appear under specific NOS codes tracking extraordinary relief.

definition: An extraordinary writ compelling a government official or court to perform a mandatory duty or correcting an abuse of discretion.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 1361 provides federal question jurisdiction; Fed. R. App. P. 21 governs petitions; available only when no other remedy exists.
dataContext: Interlocutory remedy; separate appeal mechanism.

definition: A pre-trial hearing where a judge construes patent claims and determines their scope for infringement analysis.
federalContext: Named after Markman v. Westview Instruments; judge decides claim scope questions as matter of law; dispositive in patent cases.
dataContext: Markman hearings are mandatory in patent litigation.

definition: A settlement agreement between plaintiff and some defendants allowing the settling defendants to benefit financially from larger judgments against non-settling defendants.
federalContext: Disclosure required in some jurisdictions; controversial; can incentivize collusion; enforceability varies by state.
dataContext: Settlement structuring in multi-defendant cases.

definition: A burden-shifting framework for analyzing employment discrimination cases: plaintiff establishes prima facie case, defendant offers legitimate reason, plaintiff shows reason is pretext.
federalContext: Established in McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973); standard analytical framework in Title VII cases.
dataContext: Discrimination case analysis methodology.
source: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

definition: Patent claims using functional language (e.g., 'means for') interpreted under 35 U.S.C. § 112(f) to cover only the structure disclosed in the specification.
federalContext: Governed by 35 U.S.C. § 112(f) and Markman analysis; narrow interpretation; requires adequate structural disclosure.
dataContext: Claim interpretation issue in patent litigation.

definition: The middle value in a distribution; 50% of values above and 50% below; less affected by outliers than mean.
federalContext: Used in federal case statistics to describe typical damages, settlement amounts, or case durations.
dataContext: Median damages are standard case statistics metric.

definition: A non-binding alternative dispute resolution process where neutral mediator facilitates settlement negotiations between parties.
federalContext: Court-ordered or voluntary; mediator cannot impose settlement; parties retain control; protected settlement discussions.
dataContext: Mediation outcomes indicate settlement likelihood and value.

definition: In structured settlements, a fund set aside to cover future Medicare-covered expenses, preventing Medicare from subrogating against settlement proceeds.
federalContext: Required by CMS for settlements involving future medical expenses; Trust Law compliance required; specialized analysis needed.
dataContext: Medicare coordination in settlement cases.

definition: A statistical technique combining results from multiple studies to draw general conclusions about a particular question.
federalContext: Used in scientific evidence; combines studies for larger sample size; subject to Daubert reliability scrutiny.
dataContext: Statistical methodology for aggregating evidence.

definition: A trial terminated by the court due to a procedural or substantive error, requiring a new trial with a new jury.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 50(a) and common law grounds; declared when manifest necessity exists.
dataContext: Case outcome code; disposition indicating trial failure.

definition: A legal obligation requiring the injured party to take reasonable steps to minimize damages after a breach of contract or tort, with failure to mitigate potentially reducing the recovery.
federalContext: Recognized in federal courts; applied under state law in state-law claims; federal common law in federal contract disputes.
dataContext: Economic analysis of reasonable actions plaintiff could have taken to reduce losses.

definition: An employment discrimination case in which both legitimate and illegitimate reasons motivated an employment decision, analyzed under burden-shifting frameworks.
federalContext: Analyzed under Price Waterhouse v. Hopkins, 490 U.S. 228 (1989) and Title VII amendments; burden shifts based on evidence presented.
dataContext: Discrimination case analysis methodology.

definition: A doctrine rendering a case non-justiciable when the issues are no longer live or the requested relief cannot be granted.
federalContext: Bars federal court review of cases where the controversy has been resolved during litigation; exceptions include capable-of-repetition/evading review.
dataContext: Mootness dismissals indicate issue resolution before trial.

definition: A formal request to a court for a ruling or order on a particular matter during litigation.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 7; includes motions to dismiss, for summary judgment, for judgment notwithstanding verdict, and many others.
dataContext: Motion filings and dispositions are key data points in case timeline analysis.

definition: A request to dismiss a case for failure to state a claim, lack of jurisdiction, improper venue, or other procedural defects.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b) governs; under 12(b)(6), dismissal permitted for failure to state valid claim under Twombly/Iqbal standard.
dataContext: Motion to dismiss grants result in case dismissal.

definition: A federal procedure consolidating related cases pending in multiple districts before a single judge for coordination and pre-trial.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 1407 governs MDLs; established by Panel on Multidistrict Litigation when centralization will promote convenience and efficiency.
dataContext: MDL designation dramatically increases case docket size and management complexity.

definition: A numerical classification system categorizing federal court cases by substantive legal issue; enables case type analysis.
federalContext: FJC Integrated Database uses nature of suit codes; examples: 110 (diversity insurance), 310 (motor vehicle torts).
dataContext: Nature of suit codes are primary federal case classification system.

definition: Failure to exercise reasonable care, causing unintended injury; requires duty, breach, causation, and damages.
federalContext: Common law tort; federal courts in diversity apply state substantive negligence law under Erie doctrine.
dataContext: Negligence is primary basis for personal injury and tort cases.

definition: A post-trial remedy in which a trial court orders a new trial based on trial error, newly discovered evidence, or that the verdict is contrary to the weight of evidence.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 59; motion must be filed within 28 days of judgment; discretionary remedy.
dataContext: Post-trial motion; case disposition indicator.

definition: Latin for 'I will not contest'; a criminal plea equivalent to guilty for sentencing purposes but not constituting admission for civil liability.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Crim. P. 11; entered only with court approval; civil litigation impact limited.
dataContext: Criminal plea type; civil consequences restricted.

definition: Monetary compensation for intangible losses such as pain and suffering, emotional distress, and loss of enjoyment of life.
federalContext: Awarded in most cases; subject to damages caps in some jurisdictions; assessed by jury; harder to quantify.
dataContext: Damages calculation component; often disputed.

definition: A procedural requirement under the Administrative Procedure Act requiring agencies to publish proposed rules and allow public comment before finalizing regulations.
federalContext: Governed by 5 U.S.C. § 553; required for most federal agency rulemaking; failure to comply can invalidate rules.
dataContext: Administrative law litigation; regulatory challenge basis.

definition: A document filed to initiate an appeal of a final judgment; triggers appellate jurisdiction and determines appellate court.
federalContext: Fed. R. App. P. 3 requires notice of appeal within 30 days of entry of judgment; failure to file waives appeal right.
dataContext: Notice of appeal triggers appellate docket creation.

definition: The substitution of a new contract for an old one, or the substitution of a new party for an old one, with the consent of all parties and extinguishing the original obligation.
federalContext: Governed by state contract law; federal courts apply state principles of novation.
dataContext: Contract modification issue in commercial litigation.

definition: A manifestation of willingness to enter into a contract on specified terms, capable of acceptance forming binding contract.
federalContext: Offer must be clear and definite; essential contract term; distinguishable from preliminary negotiations.
dataContext: Offer disputes affect contract formation timing.
