definition: A settlement offer made in accordance with Fed. R. Civ. P. 68 that triggers cost-shifting if rejected and the offeree fails to obtain a better result.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 68; offeree pays costs if judgment obtained is not more favorable; incentivizes settlement.
dataContext: Settlement mechanism with cost consequences.

definition: A hearing where appellate lawyers present arguments to a panel of judges regarding appeal issues.
federalContext: Fed. R. App. P. 34 governs oral argument; not all cases granted oral argument; presented by counsel to appellate panel.
dataContext: Oral argument grants indicate significant appeal cases.

definition: The probability of obtaining observed results (or more extreme) if the null hypothesis were true, used to assess statistical significance.
federalContext: Used in scientific evidence; p-value less than 0.05 typically threshold for significance; subject to Daubert scrutiny.
dataContext: Statistical significance measurement.

definition: Non-economic damages compensating for physical pain, mental anguish, emotional distress, and loss of enjoyment of life.
federalContext: Recoverable in tort cases; no fixed formula; jury may consider severity, duration, and impact on plaintiff's life.
dataContext: Pain and suffering awards vary significantly by case and jury.

definition: A group of judges (usually three) from an appellate court hearing and deciding a case.
federalContext: Circuit court panels typically consist of three judges; panel decisions establish circuit precedent.
dataContext: Panel composition may affect decision outcomes.

definition: Oral or extrinsic evidence offered to modify, contradict, or supplement the terms of a written contract.
federalContext: Governed by state contract law; federal courts apply parol evidence rule under applicable state law; Fed. R. Evid. 401-403 govern admissibility.
dataContext: Key issue in contract interpretation disputes.

definition: An exception to the hearsay rule allowing a witness to testify based on a writing made by the witness when the matter was fresh in the witness's mind.
federalContext: Codified in Fed. R. Evid. 803(5); the writing itself is not admitted but witness can testify to its contents.
dataContext: Used when witness cannot recall facts but contemporaneous writing exists.

definition: Unauthorized making, using, selling, or offering for sale of patented invention during patent term.
federalContext: 35 U.S.C. § 271 defines infringement; federal question jurisdiction; damages include lost profits and reasonable royalty.
dataContext: Patent cases involve specialized litigation and higher damages.

definition: Latin for 'pending the litigation'; temporary orders made during divorce or other pending litigation affecting family relationships.
federalContext: Limited federal application; primarily state domestic law; temporary support and custody orders.
dataContext: Family law litigation context.

definition: A method of calculating non-economic damages by assigning a daily monetary value to pain and suffering for each day of injury or disability.
federalContext: Common trial argument; jury sets per diem rate; multiplied by number of days; jurors must agree on rate and duration.
dataContext: Non-economic damages calculation methodology.

definition: A statistical measure indicating the percentage of observations below a given value; 75th percentile means 75% of values are below it.
federalContext: Used in federal case statistics to describe distribution of damages and settlement amounts.
dataContext: Percentiles describe settlement and damages distributions.

definition: The right of an attorney to challenge and remove a prospective juror without stating a reason, limited to a specific number set by rule.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 47(b); limited number; cannot be used on basis of race, gender, or other protected status (Batson challenge).
dataContext: Jury selection tool; tracked in voir dire records.

definition: Structured settlement payments made at regular intervals rather than as a lump sum, providing ongoing income to plaintiff.
federalContext: Common in catastrophic injury cases; can include cost-of-living adjustments; funded through annuities.
dataContext: Settlement payment structure.

definition: Legal authority that courts may consider but need not follow; includes decisions from other jurisdictions and secondary sources.
federalContext: Decisions from other circuits, foreign courts, and law review articles are persuasive authority.
dataContext: Persuasive authority supports legal arguments.

definition: In criminal law, a defendant's response to criminal charges (guilty, not guilty, or nolo contendere); in civil law, a response or defense raised in a pleading.
federalContext: Criminal pleas governed by Fed. R. Crim. P. 11; civil pleas governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 7.
dataContext: Case disposition tracked by plea type in federal criminal cases.

definition: A formal written allegation or response filed in court; includes complaint, answer, and reply.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 7 limits pleadings to complaint, answer, reply to counterclaim, third-party complaint, and third-party answer.
dataContext: Pleadings establish legal and factual framework of case.

definition: A legal proceeding challenging a conviction on grounds such as ineffective assistance of counsel or constitutional violations.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 2255 provides federal post-conviction remedy for federal prisoners; § 2254 for state prisoners.
dataContext: Post-conviction cases appear in prisoner litigation docket.

definition: An administrative proceeding before the USPTO PTAB in which any party can challenge patent validity on any statutory ground within nine months of grant.
federalContext: Governed by 35 U.S.C. § 321-329; narrower time window than IPR; grounds include subject matter eligibility and written description.
dataContext: Patent validity challenges; early validity attack mechanism.

definition: Motions filed after trial disposition requesting the court to set aside the verdict, enter judgment as matter of law, or grant a new trial.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 50, 59, 60; must be filed within 28 days of judgment; include JNOV and new trial motions.
dataContext: Post-trial remedies; case outcome modification.

definition: A prior court decision that guides or controls later cases; binding authority in same jurisdiction.
federalContext: Circuit court decisions are binding precedent within circuit; Supreme Court decisions are binding nationwide.
dataContext: Precedent guides legal arguments and case outcome predictions.

definition: Evidence likely to cause unfair bias or emotional reaction in a jury, influencing verdict improperly.
federalContext: Fed. R. Evid. 403 permits exclusion when probative value is substantially outweighed by danger of unfair prejudice.
dataContext: Prejudicial evidence rulings affect trial outcomes and appeal grounds.

definition: An injunction granted before final judgment, requiring one party to cease or continue an act pending trial on the merits.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 65(a); requires likelihood of success, irreparable harm, balance of equities, and public interest under Winter v. Natural Resources Defense Council.
dataContext: Preliminary injunction grants are strong indicators of plaintiff strength and case significance.

definition: An exception to the hearsay rule allowing statements describing an event or condition made while the declarant was perceiving it or immediately thereafter.
federalContext: Codified in Fed. R. Evid. 803(1); requires contemporaneous description of the event.
dataContext: Narrower exception than excited utterance; timing is critical.

definition: A false reason or justification offered by an employer to mask the real (discriminatory) reason for an employment decision.
federalContext: Analyzed in discrimination cases under McDonnell Douglas framework; can be inferred from circumstances.
dataContext: Discrimination case element; evidence of discriminatory intent.

definition: A meeting between the parties and judge held before trial to address remaining issues, settlement possibilities, and trial preparation.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 16; judge may require attendance; results in final pretrial order or settlement discussion.
dataContext: Trial preparation milestone.

definition: A case in which the plaintiff has presented sufficient evidence to prove each element of the claim unless rebutted by the defendant.
federalContext: Required to survive motion to dismiss and summary judgment; burden shifts through litigation stages under summary judgment standard.
dataContext: Proof standard; applies across all case types.

definition: A doctrine requiring courts to defer to an administrative agency's expertise on certain issues before exercising judicial jurisdiction.
federalContext: Common law doctrine applied by federal courts; issue-specific; court retains ultimate jurisdiction.
dataContext: Administrative law jurisdiction issue.

definition: Existing inventions, publications, or disclosures demonstrating that patent claims lack novelty or are obvious.
federalContext: 35 U.S.C. § 102 defines prior art; patent examiner considers prior art in patentability analysis.
dataContext: Prior art references are central to patent prosecution and litigation.

definition: A legal right or immunity protecting certain communications from disclosure, such as attorney-client or physician-patient privilege.
federalContext: Fed. R. Evid. 501 applies state law privilege in diversity cases; privileges protect sensitive communications from discovery.
dataContext: Privilege claims and disputes drive discovery disputes and protective orders.

definition: Latin for 'for the public good'; professional work done without charge, typically by attorneys for clients who cannot afford legal fees.
federalContext: Encouraged by bar associations; attorney fee-shifting statutes sometimes award fees for pro bono work; ethical obligation.
dataContext: Legal services provision model.

definition: Latin for 'for this occasion'; a request to permit an attorney licensed in another jurisdiction to practice in a specific case.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. App. P. 46 and local rules; common in multi-state litigation; requires motion and approval.
dataContext: Attorney licensing exception for specific cases.

definition: Latin for 'for oneself'; representing oneself without an attorney in a legal proceeding.
federalContext: Parties have right to pro se representation; federal courts do not give special procedural treatment; subject to same rules.
dataContext: Self-representation in litigation.

definition: Evidence tending to prove or disprove a fact of consequence; having probative force.
federalContext: Fed. R. Evid. 403 allows exclusion of probative evidence if probative value substantially outweighed by danger of unfair prejudice.
dataContext: Probative value assessments affect evidence presentation strategy.

definition: Legal responsibility of manufacturers and sellers for injuries caused by defective or dangerous products.
federalContext: Theories include negligence, breach of warranty, and strict liability; varies by product and injury type.
dataContext: Product liability cases often involve multiple parties and high damages.

definition: An extraordinary writ preventing a court or official from exercising jurisdiction it does not possess or from proceeding in excess of authority.
federalContext: Governed by common law writ principles; available in federal courts; Fed. R. App. P. 21.
dataContext: Rare remedy; interlocutory jurisdiction issues.

definition: An equitable doctrine preventing a party from denying the existence of a contract when another party reasonably relies on a promise and would suffer injustice if the promise is breached.
federalContext: Applied by federal courts under applicable state law; Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 90 widely adopted.
dataContext: Alternative theory of contract formation when consideration is lacking.

definition: A doctrine preventing a patentee from asserting claim scope under the doctrine of equivalents to cover subject matter abandoned during patent prosecution.
federalContext: Established in multiple Supreme Court decisions; limits equivalents analysis based on statements made during prosecution.
dataContext: Patent infringement defense; scope limitation.

definition: A group of persons with a shared characteristic protected from discrimination by law, including race, color, religion, sex, and national origin.
federalContext: Title VII (race, color, religion, sex, national origin), ADEA (age 40+), ADA (disability), GINA (genetics).
dataContext: Protected class status is required element in discrimination claims.

definition: A court order protecting parties or non-parties from discovery of sensitive information, restricting use of discovered material to litigation.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(c); requires good cause; common in commercial litigation to protect trade secrets.
dataContext: Discovery confidentiality mechanism.

definition: A filing with the USPTO establishing an early filing date for a patent application while allowing time to develop and test the invention before filing a regular application.
federalContext: Governed by 35 U.S.C. § 111(b); one-year priority period; not examined; becomes abandoned if non-provisional not filed within one year.
dataContext: Patent prosecution milestone; filing date important for prior art.

definition: The legal cause of injury; requires that defendant's negligence be a substantial factor in producing harm and not too remote.
federalContext: Required for tort liability; assessed using foreseeability and policy considerations; distinct from but-for causation.
dataContext: Proximate cause disputes affect tort liability determinations.

definition: Damages awarded beyond compensation to punish defendant for egregious conduct and deter similar behavior.
federalContext: Subject to constitutional limits under BMW v. Gore and State Farm v. Campbell; must not be excessive relative to actual harm.
dataContext: Punitive damages awards indicate severe misconduct findings.

definition: A defense protecting government officials from liability unless they violate clearly established constitutional rights.
federalContext: Established in Harlow v. Fitzgerald; two-prong test: constitutional violation and clearly established right; absolute immunity for some officials.
dataContext: Qualified immunity defenses result in early case dismissal.

definition: A trust or escrow account holding settlement proceeds and tax-deferred earnings to fund structured settlements, with favorable tax treatment.
federalContext: Governed by IRC § 468B; allows tax deferral of settlement earnings; structured settlement vehicle; professional administration required.
dataContext: Settlement funding mechanism; tax-advantaged structure.

definition: Latin phrase meaning 'as much as is deserved'; an equitable remedy allowing recovery of the reasonable value of services or goods provided when no enforceable contract exists.
federalContext: Applied by federal courts in unjust enrichment cases; governed by state law; also available in federal contract disputes.
dataContext: Alternative basis for recovery when express contract is unenforceable.

definition: A legal action brought by a private citizen (relator) on behalf of the government under the False Claims Act, with relator entitled to portion of recovery.
federalContext: 31 U.S.C. § 3730; private parties sue on behalf of United States; relator receives percentage of recovery; sealed complaints.
dataContext: Whistleblower fraud case type; federal question jurisdiction.

definition: A legal action challenging the authority or qualification of a person or entity holding a public office or position.
federalContext: Governed by common law principles; 28 U.S.C. § 1361; available in federal courts; addresses jurisdiction and qualifications.
dataContext: Administrative law remedy; government authority issues.

definition: An employer's obligation to modify job duties or workplace to enable qualified disabled employee to perform essential functions.
federalContext: ADA § 12111(9) requires reasonable accommodation unless causing undue hardship; examples include accessible facilities, assistive technology, modified schedules.
dataContext: Reasonable accommodation disputes are common in disability discrimination cases.

definition: An equitable remedy that corrects a written contract to reflect the true agreement of the parties when the writing does not accurately express their actual intent.
federalContext: Available in federal courts; requires clear and convincing evidence of the true agreement; governed by applicable state law.
dataContext: Equitable remedy in contract dispute cases.

definition: A statistical technique used to identify and quantify relationships between variables, commonly used in employment discrimination cases.
federalContext: Key methodology in discrimination cases; multiple regression controls for confounding variables; subject to Daubert challenge.
dataContext: Statistical analysis methodology for discrimination cases.

definition: Examination of a witness's own evidence (on redirect) to restore credibility after the witness has been impeached by the opposing party.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Evid. 706; limited to undoing the specific damage caused by impeachment.
dataContext: Follows cross-examination in trial presentation.

definition: A procedure allowing a patentee to correct errors or defects in a patent by surrendering it and obtaining a new patent with corrected claims.
federalContext: Governed by 35 U.S.C. § 251; must be filed during patent term; cannot expand scope materially or beyond two years of original grant.
dataContext: Patent correction mechanism; limited scope expansion.

definition: Evidence having a direct bearing on the facts in dispute; logically connected to prove or disprove a matter.
federalContext: Fed. R. Evid. 401 defines relevance; 402 makes relevant evidence admissible unless otherwise specified.
dataContext: Relevance determinations affect trial presentation and jury decision-making.

definition: An appellate court's order sending a case back to the trial court for further proceedings or a new trial.
federalContext: Common appellate remedy when trial court erred; may remand with or without instructions.
dataContext: Remand rates indicate appellate court confidence in lower court procedures.

definition: An order returning a removed case to the state court from which it was removed.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 1447 governs remand; remand is proper if federal court lacks subject matter jurisdiction.
dataContext: Remand rates indicate technical removal issues and jurisdictional challenges.

definition: The transfer of a case from state court to federal court, typically based on federal question or diversity jurisdiction.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 1441 governs removal; must be filed within 30 days of service and cannot be removed after one year unless lack of federal jurisdiction was fraudulent.
dataContext: Removal rates indicate prevalence of diversity and federal question cases.

definition: Latin phrase meaning 'the thing speaks for itself'; a doctrine allowing a plaintiff to establish negligence circumstantially when an accident would not have occurred absent negligence and the instrumentality was under exclusive control of the defendant.
federalContext: Applied in federal courts under applicable state law; requires expert testimony in many jurisdictions.
dataContext: Common in medical malpractice and product liability cases.

definition: A doctrine preventing re-litigation of a claim that was decided on the merits by a final judgment.
federalContext: Applies in federal court when: (1) prior final judgment, (2) same parties, (3) same cause of action.
dataContext: Res judicata dismissals indicate prior litigation history or duplicate filings.

definition: An equitable remedy that cancels a contract and restores the parties to their original position before the contract was formed.
federalContext: Available in federal courts for fraud, misrepresentation, breach, and other grounds; governed by applicable state law.
dataContext: Equitable remedy tracked separately from damages claims.

definition: A catch-all exception to the hearsay rule (Fed. R. Evid. 807) allowing hearsay to be admitted if it has circumstantial guarantees of trustworthiness and is material to the case.
federalContext: Codified in Fed. R. Evid. 807; requires notice to opposing party; rarely granted; addresses gap-filling.
dataContext: Limited use exception; applies when specific exception does not fit.

definition: An employer's adverse employment action taken against an employee in response to protected activity such as filing a discrimination complaint.
federalContext: Title VII, ADEA, and ADA prohibit retaliation; temporal proximity and causation establish link between protected activity and adverse action.
dataContext: Retaliation claims frequently accompany discrimination allegations.

definition: An appellate court's decision to overturn a lower court judgment; judgment vacated and case often remanded.
federalContext: Reversal requires showing clear error or abuse of discretion; appellate court must identify reversible error.
dataContext: Reversal rates indicate trial court error frequency.

definition: A notice from EEOC certifying that administrative remedies are exhausted and permitting filing of federal court civil rights lawsuit.
federalContext: Required to file Title VII discrimination suit under 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-5(f); EEOC issues after investigation or 180 days of charge filing.
dataContext: Right-to-sue letters are mandatory predicate for employment discrimination litigation.

definition: A doctrine requiring that a case be sufficiently developed and the injury sufficiently imminent before federal court jurisdiction attaches.
federalContext: Addressed in Abbott Labs v. Gardner; prevents premature federal adjudication of hypothetical disputes.
dataContext: Ripeness dismissals indicate premature claims or unripe requests for relief.

definition: A formal settlement offer made under Fed. R. Civ. P. 68 for a specified amount, with cost-shifting consequences if rejected.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 68; must specify damages precisely; costs assessed against non-acceptor if judgment not more favorable.
dataContext: Settlement incentive mechanism.

definition: The federal agency process for promulgating regulations; includes notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA.
federalContext: 5 U.S.C. § 553 requires notice, opportunity for comment, and publication of final rule; subject to arbitrary-and-capricious review.
dataContext: Rulemaking petitions and disputes appear in administrative docket.

definition: The number of observations in a sample; larger samples provide more reliable statistical estimates.
federalContext: Federal case statistics based on FJIC data; sample size affects reliability of findings.
dataContext: Sample size determines statistical reliability of case data.

definition: A penalty imposed by a court for violation of rules, court orders, or abuse of litigation process.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 sanctions for frivolous pleadings; Rule 37 sanctions for discovery violations.
dataContext: Sanction orders track litigation conduct violations.

definition: A conference between the parties and judge under Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(f) to discuss case management, discovery, and case development plan.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(f) and 16; required within 90 days of complaint; results in scheduling order.
dataContext: Case management milestone; early case assessment.

definition: Federal statute providing rights to make and enforce contracts regardless of race, creating basis for employment discrimination claims in federal court.
federalContext: 42 U.S.C. § 1981; applicable to all employment contexts; no employer size threshold; damages include compensatory and punitive damages.
dataContext: Employment discrimination case type; federal question jurisdiction.

definition: A federal civil rights statute providing remedy for deprivation of constitutional or federal statutory rights by persons acting under color of state law.
federalContext: 42 U.S.C. § 1983 permits suit against government officials; does not provide private right of action against private parties.
dataContext: Section 1983 cases are major federal docket component.

definition: Internal Revenue Code provision allowing settlement proceeds to be held in a qualified settlement fund with tax deferral for earnings.
federalContext: Governed by IRC § 468B and related regulations; applies to structured settlements; enables tax-efficient settlement structuring.
dataContext: Tax law provision for settlement funding.

definition: A systematic error occurring when the sample is not representative of the population, affecting the validity of statistical conclusions.
federalContext: Challenge to statistical evidence; affects reliability of expert opinions; Daubert issue.
dataContext: Statistical methodology challenge.

definition: The delivery of a summons and complaint or other court document to a defendant, establishing the court's jurisdiction over that person.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 4 requires service within 90 days of filing; Rule 4(m) prescribes methods of service.
dataContext: Service status is tracked to determine case viability and jurisdictional sufficiency.

definition: A counterclaim or cross-demand that reduces or extinguishes an opponent's claim by the amount owed by the opponent.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 13; compulsory counterclaim may be setoff; reduces net judgment.
dataContext: Damages mitigation mechanism.

definition: An agreement between parties resolving disputed claims without trial; typically confidential and includes release of claims.
federalContext: Settlements may be confidential or public; subject to Rule 68 settlement offer provisions; documented in settlement agreements.
dataContext: Settlement rates indicate litigation resolution; high settlement rates suggest reasonable case valuation.

definition: The expected range of settlement values for similar cases based on comparable case outcomes and damages.
federalContext: Determined by benchmarking similar cases; assists in settlement negotiations and case valuation.
dataContext: Settlement ranges guide settlement discussions.

definition: A court order separating claims, issues, or parties that were joined, typically to prevent prejudice or promote fairness.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 42(b); court discretion; balances efficiency against fairness concerns.
dataContext: Case management tool; opposite of consolidation.

definition: Latin for 'without which not'; an essential condition or prerequisite; used in legal analysis for causation analysis.
federalContext: Used in federal liability analysis; but-for causation test; establishes necessary link between conduct and harm.
dataContext: Causation analysis framework.

definition: A doctrine giving lesser deference to agency interpretations not based on formal processes, examined for thoroughness, logic, and consistency.
federalContext: Established in Skidmore v. Swift & Co., 323 U.S. 134 (1944); applies to informal agency guidance; fact-specific.
dataContext: Administrative law standard of review.

definition: Damages for economic losses and out-of-pocket expenses; must be specifically pleaded and proven with particularity.
federalContext: Must be listed in complaint or else waived; includes medical expenses, lost wages, and economic losses.
dataContext: Special damages are more readily calculated than general damages.

definition: An equitable remedy requiring party to perform contract obligations rather than pay damages; available when money damages inadequate.
federalContext: Equitable remedy available at court discretion; requires feasible performance, no undue hardship, and inadequacy of damages.
dataContext: Specific performance awards appear in property and unique goods cases.

definition: A measure of statistical dispersion showing how spread out data values are from the mean or average value.
federalContext: Used in statistical analysis; multiple standard deviations from mean indicate potential discrimination in some analyses.
dataContext: Statistical measurement methodology.

definition: A party's right to bring or maintain a lawsuit, requiring injury-in-fact, causation, and redressability.
federalContext: Article III requires concrete injury; established in Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife; jurisdictional requirement in federal courts.
dataContext: Standing challenges often result in case dismissal early in litigation.

definition: An order issued by a judge that applies generally to all cases in the judge's courtroom or to a specific category of cases.
federalContext: Administrative orders governing litigation procedures; must comply with Federal Rules; applies to all affected cases.
dataContext: Court procedural governance.

definition: The doctrine of following precedent; courts adhere to prior decisions to maintain consistency and predictability.
federalContext: Vertical stare decisis: lower courts bound by higher court precedent; horizontal stare decisis: courts follow own precedent.
dataContext: Stare decisis ensures predictable case outcomes.

definition: Latin for 'let the decision stand'; the doctrine of precedent requiring courts to follow prior decisions of higher courts and their own prior decisions.
federalContext: Binding precedent is mandatory; persuasive authority is discretionary; federal courts must follow Supreme Court and circuit precedent.
dataContext: Core legal principle; governs judicial decision-making.

definition: A measure of whether observed differences in data are likely due to actual differences rather than random chance.
federalContext: Used in discrimination cases and scientific evidence; p-value less than 0.05 often threshold; Daubert scrutiny required.
dataContext: Statistical evidence standard.

definition: A legal principle that certain contracts must be evidenced by a written document signed by the party against whom the contract is being enforced to be enforceable.
federalContext: Varies by state; federal courts apply state statute of frauds; common areas include real estate, marriage, one-year performance.
dataContext: Jurisdictional threshold issue in contract enforcement cases.

definition: A law establishing the time period within which a claim must be brought, after which the right to sue is forfeited.
federalContext: Federal courts apply state statute of limitations in diversity cases under 28 U.S.C. § 1652 (Erie doctrine); federal statutes of limitations apply in federal question cases.
dataContext: Statute of limitations expiration results in dismissal of time-barred claims.

definition: A court order halting or suspending proceedings or enforcement of a judgment, typically pending appeal or other resolution.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 62(a) stays execution of judgment automatically during appeal period; stays pending appeal require security under Rule 62(d).
dataContext: Stay filings indicate intention to appeal and litigation continuation.

definition: A dismissal of a lawsuit agreed to by all parties, typically with or without prejudice and subject to settlement terms.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 41(a)(1)(A); requires written agreement; dismissal with prejudice bars future litigation.
dataContext: Settlement disposition; case closure mechanism.

definition: An agreement between parties to establish certain facts, admit evidence, or waive procedural requirements.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 29 permits parties to modify discovery timing and scope by stipulation; facts stipulated are established.
dataContext: Stipulations reduce discovery disputes and trial issues.

definition: Legal liability without requiring proof of negligence or intent; defendant is liable for harm regardless of care exercised.
federalContext: Applied to abnormally dangerous activities, defective products, and ultra-hazardous conditions.
dataContext: Strict liability theory appears in product liability and casualty cases.

definition: A settlement providing periodic payments over time rather than lump sum; often involves annuity for tax benefits.
federalContext: Common in personal injury cases; Section 104(a)(2) excludes periodic payments from gross income under IRC.
dataContext: Structured settlements indicate risk management in high-value cases.

definition: A settlement agreement in which damages are paid to the plaintiff through periodic installments rather than a lump sum.
federalContext: Often funded through annuities; tax treatment favorable under IRC § 104; popular in personal injury cases.
dataContext: Settlement payment structure; damages distribution mechanism.

definition: Latin for 'under a judge'; a matter pending before a court, which cannot be discussed publicly due to sub judice contempt rules.
federalContext: Limited application in U.S. federal law; free speech protections limit contempt liability; contrast with parliamentary systems.
dataContext: Litigation pendency indicator.

definition: A court order commanding a person to testify or produce evidence under penalty of contempt.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 45 governs issuance, service, and enforcement of subpoenas in federal court.
dataContext: Subpoena compliance often noted in case progression tracking.

definition: A creditor's or insurer's right to be substituted for the debtor or plaintiff to pursue the debtor's or plaintiff's rights.
federalContext: Health insurance subrogation rights appear in settlement disputes; state law varies on subrogation rights.
dataContext: Subrogation claims reduce plaintiff recovery.

definition: Evidence that a party made repairs or improvements after an accident, generally inadmissible to prove negligence or liability, though admissible for other purposes.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Evid. 407; policy exclusion to encourage remedial actions; many exceptions apply.
dataContext: Key admissibility issue in product liability and negligence cases.

definition: A standard of judicial review requiring that agency action be supported by such relevant evidence as a reasonable person would accept as adequate to support a conclusion.
federalContext: Governed by 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(E); applies to formal agency proceedings; higher bar than arbitrary and capricious.
dataContext: Administrative law standard of review.

definition: A doctrine allowing a party to recover for contract performance even when performance is not strictly literal, provided the party substantially performed the contract conditions.
federalContext: Applied in federal courts under applicable state contract law; allows recovery despite minor deviations.
dataContext: Key issue in construction and services contracts.

definition: A judgment granted before trial when evidence shows no genuine dispute of material fact and one party is entitled to judgment as a matter of law.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 56; moving party must demonstrate absence of genuine issue; burden shifts to non-movant under Celotex Corp. v. Catrett.
dataContext: Summary judgment rates indicate litigation efficiency and case complexity.

definition: A document issued by a court directing a defendant to appear and respond to a complaint or face default judgment.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 4 prescribes methods of service and requirements for valid summons in federal court.
dataContext: Service of summons is the first tracked event in case inception.

definition: The highest court in the United States; nine justices; reviews cases of national importance through certiorari.
federalContext: Supreme Court has discretionary jurisdiction over most cases; decisions establish binding precedent nationwide.
dataContext: Supreme Court decisions are landmark precedents affecting all federal courts.

definition: A claim that allows a decedent's estate to recover damages for injuries or losses suffered by the decedent before death, including pain and suffering and lost wages.
federalContext: Governed by state survival statutes; federal courts apply state law; matters of federal interest governed by 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a) and related provisions.
dataContext: Tracked separately from wrongful death claims in litigation databases.

definition: A statistical error occurring when analysis includes only successful cases while excluding unsuccessful cases, creating misleading conclusions.
federalContext: Challenge to statistical evidence; can skew expert opinions; Daubert issue; common in economic and medical data.
dataContext: Statistical methodology challenge.

definition: The process by which a court officer (clerk or judge) determines which costs claimed in a bill of costs are allowable and properly taxable.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 54(d); clerk taxes most routine costs; complex cost questions may go to judge.
dataContext: Cost award determination process.

definition: A court order issued without notice to the opposing party to restrain conduct pending a hearing on a preliminary injunction.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 65(b) limits TROs to 14 days without consent; designed to preserve status quo pending full hearing.
dataContext: TRO grants indicate emergency circumstances and immediate litigation stakes.

definition: A pleading by which a defendant or other party brings a non-party into the lawsuit by claiming the third party is liable for all or part of the original claim.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 14; must be timely filed; third party can assert counterclaims against defendant.
dataContext: Initiates impleader process; expands litigation.

term: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964
definition: Federal statute prohibiting employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin; creates right to sue in federal court.
federalContext: 42 U.S.C. § 2000e et seq.; enforced by EEOC; private right of action; damages available.
dataContext: Employment discrimination case type; federal question jurisdiction.

definition: The suspension of the statute of limitations period due to specified circumstances, extending the deadline to file suit.
federalContext: Doctrines include equitable tolling, fraudulent concealment, and minority; varies by claim type and applicable law.
dataContext: Tolling arguments appear in statute of limitations disputes.

definition: The distinctive appearance, design, or packaging of a product or service that identifies and distinguishes it from competitors, protectable as trademark.
federalContext: Governed by 15 U.S.C. § 1127; requires distinctiveness and non-functionality; two types: inherent and acquired secondary meaning.
dataContext: Intellectual property protection; tracked in trademark litigation.

definition: Valuable business information not generally known giving competitive advantage; protected from misappropriation under UTSA.
federalContext: 18 U.S.C. § 1836 provides federal remedy for trade secret misappropriation; UTSA adopted by most states.
dataContext: Trade secret cases involve confidential business information.

definition: A mark, word, phrase, or symbol identifying goods or services and distinguishing them from competitors.
federalContext: 15 U.S.C. § 1127 defines trademark; Lanham Act provides federal registration and infringement protections; likelihood of confusion standard.
dataContext: Trademark cases involve brand protection and consumer confusion.

definition: Damages awarded at three times the amount of actual damages; statutory remedy in specific cases like antitrust and patent.
federalContext: 15 U.S.C. § 15 provides treble damages in antitrust cases; available in patent, copyright, and other federal statutes.
dataContext: Treble damages awards indicate willful violation findings.

definition: A formal judicial proceeding where evidence is presented and facts determined; outcome decided by judge or jury.
federalContext: Federal rules govern trial procedure; most federal cases settle before trial; trial results establish damages and liability.
dataContext: Trial rates and outcomes are key federal case statistics.

definition: A written document filed before trial providing the court with the party's legal arguments, statement of facts, and proposed jury instructions.
federalContext: Required in many jurisdictions; submitted before trial; assists judge in understanding parties' positions and relevant law.
dataContext: Trial preparation document.

definition: A standard requiring complaint allegations to provide plausible claim for relief, not merely possible; established in Bell Atlantic v. Twombly.
federalContext: Applied under Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6); refined in Ashcroft v. Iqbal; rejects notice pleading standard.
dataContext: Twombly/Iqbal standard affects pleading adequacy in motion to dismiss practice.

definition: Latin for 'beyond powers'; an act by an agent, officer, or corporation exceeding the authority granted by law or charter.
federalContext: Challenges to agency action; administrative law; establishes lack of authority; grounds for invalidation.
dataContext: Authority limitation doctrine.

definition: A contract term or entire contract so one-sided and unfair that enforcement would be unjust; defense to contract enforcement.
federalContext: Procedural unconscionability: unfair bargaining process; substantive unconscionability: unreasonable contract terms.
dataContext: Unconscionability defenses rarely succeed in federal court.

definition: Substantial cost or operational difficulty that makes reasonable accommodation unreasonable for an employer.
federalContext: ADA requires accommodation unless causing undue hardship; assessed under factors including cost and operational impact.
dataContext: Undue hardship defense appears in accommodation dispute litigation.

definition: A patent protecting an invention's function or utility, covering methods, compositions, and machines that satisfy novelty, non-obviousness, and utility requirements.
federalContext: Governed by 35 U.S.C. § 101-103; primary patent type; 20-year term from filing; infringement litigated in federal court.
dataContext: Intellectual property protection; tracked in patent litigation.

definition: A measure of statistical dispersion equal to the square of the standard deviation, measuring spread of data around the mean.
federalContext: Statistical concept; related to standard deviation; used in regression and other analyses.
dataContext: Statistical measurement methodology.

definition: The geographic location or district in which a court has authority to hear and determine a case.
federalContext: 28 U.S.C. § 1391 establishes federal venue; generally proper in district where defendant resides, substantial events occurred, or property located.
dataContext: Venue challenges affect case progression timing and location.

definition: The formal decision rendered by a jury (or judge in a bench trial) on the issues submitted for determination.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 49 governs special verdicts and general verdicts with interrogatories.
dataContext: Verdict outcomes and damages awarded are key case resolution metrics.

definition: Legal responsibility of an employer or principal for the negligent or wrongful acts of an employee or agent committed within the scope of employment or agency.
federalContext: No federal vicarious liability statute; federal courts apply state common law on this doctrine.
dataContext: Employment and agency relationships tracked in employment discrimination cases.

definition: The examination of prospective jurors by attorneys and the court to determine bias, qualifications, and suitability to serve on a jury.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 47; scope controlled by trial judge; jurors examined on voir dire before seating.
dataContext: Jury selection stage; tracked in jury composition records.

definition: French for 'to see and hear'; examination of prospective jurors to determine suitability and identify bias.
federalContext: Governed by Fed. R. Civ. P. 47; controlled by trial judge; critical jury selection mechanism.
dataContext: Jury selection process.

term: Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) Act
definition: Federal statute requiring employers with 100+ employees to provide 60 days' notice of plant closings or mass layoffs.
federalContext: 29 U.S.C. § 2101 et seq.; enforced by Department of Labor; private right of action; damages for lost wages and benefits.
dataContext: Mass layoff dispute; federal question jurisdiction.

definition: An employee who reports illegal conduct, safety violations, or other wrongdoing by an employer, protected from retaliation under various federal and state statutes.
federalContext: Protected under SOX, Dodd-Frank, OSH Act, and other statutes; private right of action; damages for retaliation.
dataContext: Whistleblower protection case type; federal question jurisdiction.

definition: Intentional patent infringement with knowledge of the patent, potentially resulting in enhanced damages up to treble the actual damages.
federalContext: Established in Halo Electronics, Inc. v. Pulse Electronics, Inc., 831 F.3d 1369 (Fed. Cir. 2016); requires knowledge and deliberate indifference.
dataContext: Patent damage multiplier; enhanced damages issue.
source: Halo Electronics, Inc. v. Pulse Electronics, Inc., 831 F.3d 1369 (Fed. Cir. 2016)

definition: The percentage of cases resulting in favorable outcome; metric tracking litigation success rates by counsel, district, or case type.
federalContext: Measured as percentage of cases with favorable verdict or settlement; varies by practice area and counsel.
dataContext: Win rates are key case outcome metrics.

definition: Materials prepared by or for an attorney in anticipation of litigation, protected from discovery.
federalContext: Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(b)(3) protects attorney work product unless party shows substantial need and unavailability through other means.
dataContext: Work product claims are common in discovery disputes.

definition: A tort claim brought by decedent's heirs for damages resulting from defendant's negligence or wrongful conduct causing death.
federalContext: Governed by state wrongful death statutes; federal courts in diversity apply state law; recoverable by spouse, children, parents.
dataContext: Wrongful death claims involve high damage awards.

definition: A lawsuit brought by the representative of a deceased person's estate or by designated beneficiaries against a party whose wrongful conduct caused the death.
federalContext: Governed by state wrongful death statutes; federal courts apply state law; can be brought in diversity jurisdiction.
dataContext: Key damages claim in catastrophic injury cases; tracked separately from survival actions.

definition: Unlawful dismissal of an employee, whether due to discrimination, retaliation, breach of contract, or violation of public policy.
federalContext: Federal statutory protections include Title VII, ADEA, ADA, FMLA, WARN Act; also common law claims for public policy violations.
dataContext: Wrongful termination claims are among most common employment disputes.
